title: MetaFileService.read() silently returns empty config on error
description:
## Current State

MetaFileService.read() catches all errors (missing file, bad JSON, permission denied) and silently returns { upstreams: {} }. A corrupted meta.json is indistinguishable from an empty repo — skills vanish without explanation, sync does nothing silently, debugging requires tracing the entire pipeline.

## Desired State

MetaFileService.read() surfaces errors explicitly. Callers handle the error case. At minimum: log the error before falling back. Better: return a typed Effect so callers must consciously decide what to do on error.

## Implementation Notes

- Change read() to return Effect with a typed error
- Define error variants (FileNotFound, InvalidJson, PermissionDenied)
- Update all callers to handle the error explicitly
acceptance_criteria:
- MetaFileService.read() returns Effect with a typed error
- Distinct error variants for missing file, bad JSON, permission denied
- Callers handle errors explicitly (not silently swallowed)
- Corrupted meta.json produces a diagnostic message, not silent empty state

title: Define canonical Skill model in shared types
description:
## Current State

Skills are represented inconsistently across the codebase: some places use { path, hash }, others use Record<string, string>, and others use inline object literals. There is no single Skill type in shared code. The closest (DiscoveredSkill in upstream module) is not exported from shared. A Skill is a first-class domain entity defined in the project glossary — it deserves a first-class type.

## Desired State

Shared types module defines:
- Skill: { path, hash, outputName? }
- SkillHash: branded or newtype-skinned string
- SelectedSkill: { sourcePath, outputName }

All modules that represent skills use these types.

## Implementation Notes

- Define Skill, SkillHash, SelectedSkill types in shared/types/
- Update upstream, sync, clone-skills to use these types
- No behavioural changes — pure type consolidation
acceptance_criteria:
- Shared Skill type defined in shared/types/
- All skill-related data in upstream, sync, clone-skills uses the shared type
- TypeScript enforces consistent skill shapes across modules
- No behavioural change

title: Rename clone-skills command to reflect it only edits meta.json
description:
## Current State

The clone-skills command name implies it copies (clones) skills into place. In reality, it only writes the skills selection map to meta.json — the actual file copy happens during sync. The command's own output tells users to run pnpm sync to copy them, confirming the naming mismatch.

## Desired State

The command is renamed to select-skills (or integrated into sync as an interactive mode). The name accurately describes what it does: select which skills from an upstream to track.

## Implementation Notes

- Rename the module from clone-skills/ to select-skills/
- Update CLI registry, package.json scripts, and imports
- Update any documentation or help text
acceptance_criteria:
- Command renamed to accurately reflect its behaviour
- CLI registry, package.json, and imports all use the new name
- Command still produces same meta.json output
- No behavioural change — pure rename

title: Split monolithic processUpstream into named phases
description:
## Current State

The core function behind the sync command is a single 123-line Effect.gen block that handles submodule update, skill discovery, hashing, hash comparison, copy/delete/skip logic, and meta.json map updates — all together. The upstream object is mutated in place and later written back.

## Desired State

The function is split into named, testable phases:
- updateSubmodule: pull latest upstream content
- discoverCurrentSkills: walk upstream directory for SKILL.md files
- computeDiff: compare hashes, determine what to copy/remove/skip
- applyDiff: execute file copy and removal
- updateMetadata: persist updated hashes to meta.json

Each phase has a pure (or Effect-encapsulated) interface. No in-place mutation — each phase returns new state.

## Implementation Notes

- Identify phase boundaries by the data that flows between them
- Each phase should be independently testable (computeDiff does not need a real submodule)
- The sync command orchestrator becomes a sequence of these phases
acceptance_criteria:
- processUpstream is split into 5+ named phases
- Each phase has a documented input/output interface
- No in-place mutation of the upstream object
- Each phase is testable independently without full git fixtures
- All existing tests pass

title: Move shared git/skill-discovery/hash services out of upstream module
description:
## Current State

GitService, SkillDiscoveryService, and SkillHashService are defined under upstream/services/ but are consumed by sync and check modules too. The upstream/services/ barrel mixes upstream-specific exports with shared cross-cutting ones.

Sync imports from ../upstream/services/ creating an asymmetric dependency: sync -> upstream but not reverse.

## Desired State

GitService, SkillDiscoveryService, and SkillHashService live under shared/services/. The upstream/services/ barrel only exports upstream-specific items. The dependency direction is clean: sync also imports from shared/.

## Implementation Notes

- Move the 3 service files to shared/services/
- Update imports in sync, upstream, check, and any other consumers
- Ensure the upstream/services/ barrel no longer re-exports shared items
- No behavioural changes — pure relocation
acceptance_criteria:
- GitService, SkillDiscoveryService, SkillHashService live under shared/services/
- Sync imports from shared/services/, not upstream/services/
- upstream/services/ barrel only exports upstream-specific items
- All existing tests pass

title: Deduplicate MetaJson type into shared types module
description:
## Current State

The MetaJson interface (representing the meta.json config shape) is defined independently in 4 different modules: upstream, sync, clone-skills, and check. Each copy can diverge. Adding a field requires hunting down all definitions.

## Desired State

A single MetaJson type exists in a shared types module. All 4 consumers import from there. The shared type documents which fields are required vs optional, and default values.

## Implementation Notes

- Extract the MetaJson interface to a new shared location
- Update all 4 consumers to import from there
- Consider also extracting related types (UpstreamConfig, SkillEntry) that accompany MetaJson
- Verify meta.json read/write round-trips produce identical output
acceptance_criteria:
- MetaJson interface defined once in shared types
- All 4 consumer modules import from shared type
- Adding a field to MetaJson updates everywhere at compile time
- meta.json read/write round-trips produce identical output

title: Sync skips restoring deleted skill directory when hashes match
description:
## Summary

When a skill directory under `skills/` is deleted manually, running Sync does not restore it — because Sync only compares upstream hashes and skips the copy if the hash matches, without verifying the target directory exists on disk.

## Details

The bug is in `src/sync/sync.ts` at lines 115-118:

```
const existingHash = upstream.available[skillPath]
if (existingHash === upstreamHash) {
  skillsSkipped++
  continue
}
```

Hash match means "no change upstream", but it does not mean the local copy is intact. A manual `rm -rf skills/name` leaves no trace, yet Sync silently skips re-copying because the stored hash hasn't changed.

## Reproducer

1. Run Sync — a skill is copied to `skills/some-skill`
2. Run `rm -rf skills/some-skill`
3. Run Sync again — expected: skill is re-copied. Actual: sync says "skipped" and does nothing.

## Glossary note

Sync is defined as "idempotent" in the glossary. This bug violates that contract — running Sync after a manual deletion should converge to the same state as a fresh Sync.

## Fix

Before the hash-skip at line 116, check whether `targetDirectory` exists on disk. If not, fall through to the re-copy path.
notes: Fix: replaced bare hash-skip with target integrity verification via shouldSkipSkillCopy helper. On sync, after confirming the stored upstream hash matches, the target directory is also hashed using SkillHashService. Only if both match (stored hash == upstream hash == target hash) is the copy skipped. This handles deleted directories, empty directories, partial deletion, and tampered files — restoring the idempotency guarantee. 3 E2E tests added.

title: Support bare owner/repo shorthand for upstream URL
description:
Currently `pnpm upstream` requires a full URL (https://github.com/owner/repo, git@github.com:owner/repo, or github.com/owner/repo).

Wanted: `pnpm upstream kehwar/skills` should auto-expand to `https://github.com/kehwar/skills`.

Implementation: add an `owner/repo` regex to `parseGitHubUrl` in `src/upstream/upstream.ts` that expands bare `owner/repo` to `https://github.com/owner/repo`. No changes needed to `resolveUpstreamKey` or the CLI — they already handle the normalized URL and derive the upstream key from owner/repo.

Test: add test cases to `src/upstream/upstream.test.ts` for bare `owner/repo` format.
notes: Implemented in parseGitHubUrl (src/upstream/upstream.ts:36): added bareRegex /^([^/]+)\/([^/]+)$/ that expands owner/repo to https://github.com/owner/repo. The global .git strip at line 16 automatically handles owner/repo.git. Regex anchors enforce exactly one slash, rejecting multi-slash paths and bare words. 5 test cases added to parseGitHubUrl suite (src/upstream/upstream.test.ts:40-59): happy path with/without .git, multi-slash rejection, bare-word rejection. All 33 tests pass, tsc clean.

title: Build & Manifest Generation
description:
## Current State

There is no mechanism to generate the claude plugin manifest that lists all authored skills for external tool discovery.

## Desired State

Users can run `pnpm build` to scan the authored/ directory and regenerate the claude plugin manifest (.claude-plugin/plugin.json). The manifest lists all authored skills with their paths and metadata. The build process is automatically invoked whenever a new skill is created.

## User Stories

15. As a developer, I want to run pnpm build to generate the claude plugin manifest from authored/ directory, so that agents can discover my Authored Skills
16. As a developer, I want the manifest to be regenerated any time I run pnpm new-skill, so that it stays in sync without manual steps
17. As a developer, I want the manifest to list all Authored Skills with their paths, so that external tools can discover them

## Out of Scope

- Publishing manifest to remote locations
- Synced skills in the manifest (only authored skills)

## Further Notes

The manifest format should match .claude-plugin/plugin.json structure. The build command should be deterministic (same input always produces same output). The new-skill command should call build automatically after creating a skill.
acceptance_criteria:
- [x] `pnpm build` scans authored/ directory recursively
- [x] Manifest is generated/updated at .claude-plugin/marketplace.json
- [x] Manifest includes all authored skills with name and path
- [x] Manifest includes domain information where applicable
- [ ] `pnpm new-skill` automatically calls build after creating skill
- [x] Build is deterministic (idempotent)
- [x] Manifest format is JSON and valid
- [x] Handles missing .claude-plugin directory gracefully
- [x] All code passes `pnpm lint`
- [x] All code passes `pnpm typecheck`
notes: Implemented `pnpm build` command that scans authored/ and regenerates .claude-plugin/marketplace.json. Created src/build/ with build.ts (core logic), cli.ts (citty command), and tests. Registered as subcommand in cli.ts. The `new-skill` auto-call criterion is deferred since no CLI new-skill command exists yet — build() is exported for programmatic use.

title: New-skill Command
description:
## Current State

There is no command to create new authored skills. Users must manually create directories and scaffold files.

## Desired State

Users can run `pnpm new-skill <name> --domain <domain>` to scaffold a new authored skill. The command creates the folder structure, SKILL.md template, and meta.json with type: 'authored'. If a skill name exists, the user is prompted to choose a different name.

## User Stories

12. As a developer, I want to create a new Authored Skill via pnpm new-skill <name> --domain <domain>, so that I can organize and author my own Skills
13. As a developer, I want pnpm new-skill to scaffold SKILL.md and the skill store, so that I have a starting point for a new Skill
14. As a developer, I want pnpm new-skill to prompt me for a new name if the Skill already exists, so that I don't accidentally overwrite my work

## Out of Scope

- Editing skill content after creation (done by hand or external editors)
- Sharing or publishing skills to upstreams

## Further Notes

Domain is optional; if omitted, skill is created under authored/ directly. The SKILL.md should have placeholder content with inline instructions. The meta.json should include domain and optional sourceUrl fields.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `pnpm new-skill <name> --domain <domain>` creates authored/<domain>/<name>/ folder
- [ ] SKILL.md is created with template/placeholder content
- [ ] meta.json is created with type: 'authored', domain, and optional sourceUrl
- [ ] If skill name already exists, user is prompted for a new name (interactive)
- [ ] Command can be re-run with new name without issues
- [ ] Domain folder is created if it doesn't exist
- [ ] Folder structure is created recursively if needed
- [ ] All code passes `pnpm lint`
- [ ] All code passes `pnpm typecheck`

title: Cleanup Command
description:
## Current State

When skills are deselected or upstreams are removed, orphaned skill folders remain in skills/ directory. There's no cleanup mechanism.

## Desired State

Users can run `pnpm cleanup` to detect and remove synced skills that are no longer listed in meta.json. The command never touches the authored/ directory, ensuring user-authored skills are always protected.

## User Stories

11. As a developer, I want to run pnpm cleanup to automatically remove synced Skills that are no longer in the skill store, so that my skills/ directory stays clean
26. As a developer, I want synced Skills to be strictly read-only copies in skills/, so that I'm forced to fork Skills to authored/ if I want to modify them
27. As a developer, I want Authored Skills to live under authored// and be isolated from sync operations, so that my work is never overwritten

## Out of Scope

- Dry-run mode for cleanup (separate feature if needed)
- Removing authored skills or modifying authored/

## Further Notes

Cleanup should compare the contents of skills/ against the union of all selected skills across all upstreams in meta.json. Any skill in skills/ that isn't in that set should be removed. The authored/ directory must never be touched.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `pnpm cleanup` scans skills/ directory
- [ ] Identifies synced skills no longer in meta.json
- [ ] Removes orphaned skill folders from skills/
- [ ] Never modifies or removes files in authored/
- [ ] Shows which skills were removed
- [ ] Handles permission errors gracefully
- [ ] Exit code indicates success/failure
- [ ] All code passes `pnpm lint`
- [ ] All code passes `pnpm typecheck`
notes: Implemented and verified via TDD (8 tests). Core logic in src/cleanup/cleanup.ts: reads meta.json, computes declared set (union of all upstream skills.* values), diffs against skills/ top-level dirs, deletes orphans. Never touches authored/. CLI wrapper in src/cleanup/cli.ts, registered as 'cleanup' subcommand in src/cli.ts and npm script in package.json. Lint and typecheck pass.

title: Check Command
description:
## Current State

There is no command to show when each upstream is out of date relative to its declared branch.

## Desired State

Users can run `pnpm check` to see a status report for each tracked upstream, showing how many commits behind the declared branch it is. This helps users decide when to run sync.

## User Stories

10. As a developer, I want to run pnpm check to see how many commits behind each Upstream is, so that I know when to sync

## Out of Scope

- Interactive updates (check is read-only)
- Custom filtering or sorting of results

## Further Notes

The check command queries git to determine commits-behind for each upstream relative to its declared branch (or default if no branch specified). Output should be concise but readable.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] `pnpm check` lists all tracked upstreams
- [ ] For each upstream, shows commits-behind count
- [ ] Shows branch name being tracked
- [ ] Handles network errors gracefully (show status: unreachable)
- [ ] Output is concise and readable
- [ ] All code passes `pnpm lint`
- [ ] All code passes `pnpm typecheck`
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `pnpm check` lists all tracked upstreams
- [ ] For each upstream, shows commits-behind count
- [ ] Shows branch name being tracked
- [ ] Handles network errors gracefully (show status: unreachable)
- [ ] Output is concise and readable
- [ ] Exit code indicates if any upstream is significantly behind

- [ ] All code passes `pnpm typecheck`
notes: Implementation complete: pnpm check now works. Added fetchSubmodule and countCommitsBehind to GitService, created src/check/ with citty command + business logic + tests. All 54 tests pass, lint clean, typecheck clean.

title: Sync Robustness, Error Handling & Reporting
description:
## Current State

The basic sync operation doesn't handle errors gracefully or provide clear reporting of what happened.

## Desired State

The sync command is hardened with error resilience and comprehensive output reporting. If one upstream fails, sync continues processing others. Users see clear per-upstream summaries and final totals, with actionable error/warning details.

## User Stories

7. As a developer, I want pnpm sync to continue processing even if one Upstream fails, so that a single broken upstream doesn't block my workflow
9. As a developer, I want pnpm sync output to show a concise summary (per-Upstream, final totals), so that I understand what happened without overwhelming detail
18. As a developer, I want errors in any command to be clear and actionable, so that I can fix problems quickly

## Out of Scope

- Retrying failed upstreams (one-shot operation)
- Speculative lock files or transactional guarantees
- Dry-run mode (requires distinguishing real vs speculative git operations — not worth the complexity for the current use case)

## Further Notes

Output should include: upstream name, skills added/updated/removed/skipped, warnings for missing skills, error summaries. Error messages should indicate what failed and why.
acceptance_criteria:
Sync continues processing if one upstream fails
Failed upstreams are reported with error details
Output shows per-upstream summary: added/updated/removed/skipped counts
Output shows final totals across all upstreams
Missing skill warnings are clearly displayed
Error messages are actionable (e.g., 'Network timeout: <url>', 'Branch not found: <branch>')
Sync exit code reflects success/partial success/failure appropriately
All code passes pnpm lint
All code passes pnpm typecheck
notes: Scrapped --dry-run during planning grill. The core tension: a true dry-run needs to either (a) skip the git pull (giving inaccurate results since you are reporting on stale data) or (b) still pull (mutating the filesystem despite dry). Neither is acceptable for a credible dry-run, and the complexity to thread a write-guard through every Effect is not worth it for this project scale.

title: Core Sync Operation
description:
## Current State

There is no sync operation to pull upstreams and copy selected skills into the skills/ directory.

## Desired State

Users can run `pnpm sync` to perform a complete sync: pull/update all upstream submodules, checkout declared branches, discover the latest skills in each upstream, copy selected skills to skills/, and update content hashes in meta.json. The operation handles missing selected skills gracefully.

## User Stories

6. As a developer, I want to run pnpm sync to pull all Upstream branches to their correct state, discover latest Skills, and copy selected Skills to skills/, so that my local collection stays fresh
22. As a developer, I want shallow clones for all Upstreams, so that disk usage stays minimal
19. As a developer, I want the system to warn me if a Skill I selected is no longer in an Upstream, but continue processing, so that a missing Skill doesn't halt my sync
20. As a developer, I want the system to remove missing selected Skills from the skill store and delete them from skills/ during sync, so that cleanup is automatic

## Out of Scope

- Error resilience across multiple upstreams (handled in separate feature)
- Dry-run mode (handled in separate feature)
- Output reporting (handled in separate feature)
- Skill-level meta.json per synced skill (scrapped — deemed unnecessary overhead)

## Further Notes

Shallow clones should be used for all submodule operations. Missing skills should be detected and auto-removed from store and filesystem.

## Completion

All 11 remaining AC items are implemented and verified:

- `pnpm sync` pulls/updates all upstream submodules via git submodule update --remote
- Declared branches are checked out for each upstream (auto-detected if not configured)
- Latest SKILL.md files discovered via recursive directory walk in each upstream
- Selected skills are copied to skills/ directory (only when content hash differs)
- Content hashes (SHA256) calculated and stored in root meta.json per upstream
- Missing selected skills detected during discovery and removed from meta and filesystem
- Warnings issued for missing skills (non-blocking, continues processing)
- Shallow clones used (--depth 1) for all submodule operations
- `pnpm lint` and `pnpm typecheck` both pass
- 4 test files with 51 tests all passing

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [x] `pnpm sync` pulls/updates all upstream submodules
- [x] Declared branches are checked out for each upstream
- [x] Latest SKILL.md files are discovered in each upstream
- [x] Selected skills are copied to skills/ directory
- [x] Content hashes are calculated and stored in meta.json
- [x] Missing selected skills are detected during discovery
- [x] Missing skills are removed from meta.json and deleted from skills/
- [x] Warnings are issued for missing skills (non-blocking)
- [x] Shallow clones are used to minimize disk usage
- [x] All code passes `pnpm lint`
- [x] All code passes `pnpm typecheck`
- [~] Each synced skill has meta.json with type: 'synced' metadata (SCRAPPED — unnecessary)
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `pnpm sync` pulls/updates all upstream submodules
- [ ] Declared branches are checked out for each upstream
- [ ] Latest SKILL.md files are discovered in each upstream
- [ ] Selected skills are copied to skills/ directory
- [ ] Each synced skill has meta.json with type: 'synced' metadata
- [ ] Content hashes are calculated and stored in meta.json
- [ ] Missing selected skills are detected during discovery
- [ ] Missing skills are removed from meta.json and deleted from skills/
- [ ] Warnings are issued for missing skills (non-blocking)
- [ ] Shallow clones are used to minimize disk usage
- [ ] All code passes `pnpm lint`
- [ ] All code passes `pnpm typecheck`

title: Clone-skills Interactive Selector
description:
## Current State

There is no way to select which skills from an upstream to copy into the skills/ directory.

## Desired State

Users can run `pnpm clone-skills <upstream>` to see an interactive multi-select UI of available skills from that upstream. Selected skills are persisted to meta.json. The command is re-runnable, allowing users to add or remove skills over time.

## User Stories

4. As a developer, I want to run pnpm clone-skills <upstream> to get an interactive selector for which Skills to sync, so that I can choose what I need
5. As a developer, I want pnpm clone-skills to be re-runnable, allowing me to add or remove Skills from my selection, so that my choices can change over time
29. As a developer, I want cli-prompts (selectors, confirmations) to be readable and intuitive, so that interactive commands are pleasant to use

## Out of Scope

- Automatically syncing selected skills (that's the sync command)
- Managing selections across multiple upstreams (one command per upstream)

## Further Notes

The interactive selector should display available skills from meta.json (populated by upstream add). Re-running should show current selections checked. The skills field in meta.json is updated with user selections.
acceptance_criteria:
- [x] `pnpm clone-skills <upstream>` displays all available skills from that upstream
- [x] Interactive multi-select UI allows checking/unchecking skills
- [x] Currently selected skills are shown as checked
- [x] Selections are persisted to meta.json skills field
- [x] Command is fully re-runnable (can add or remove skills)
- [x] Clear UX: readable prompts, intuitive navigation
- [x] Error handling for invalid upstream names
- [x] All code passes `pnpm lint`
- [x] All code passes `pnpm typecheck`
notes:
Implemented interactive multi-select CLI using @clack/prompts. Users can run 'pnpm clone-skills <upstream>' to see an interactive selector of available skills. Currently selected skills are shown pre-checked. The command is fully re-runnable and updates meta.json on each run.

Additionally refactored shared services:
- Moved UserPromptService, MetaFileService, LogService to src/shared/services/
- Added mock factory functions (createMock*Service) for easy testing
- Upstream/services/index.ts re-exports shared services for backward compatibility
- Eliminated duplicate mock code from test files

All 38 tests pass, code is lint and typecheck clean.

title: Upstream Branch Support
description:
## Current State

The upstream add command only tracks the default branch. Users cannot specify or track non-default branches for an upstream.

## Desired State

Users can run `pnpm upstream <url> --branch <branch>` to optionally specify a branch to track for an upstream. The sync operation respects the declared branch and checks it out.

## User Stories

2. As a developer, I want to optionally specify a branch when adding an Upstream via pnpm upstream <url> --branch <branch>, so that I can track non-default branches
21. As a developer, I want each Upstream's branch to be respected during sync (checkout the declared branch), so that I can track multiple release versions

## Out of Scope

- Interactive branch selection
- Changing branches on existing upstreams (use the full command again)

## Further Notes

Branch validation should ensure the branch exists in the remote repo. The meta.json branch field is optional; omission means the repo's default branch.
acceptance_criteria:
- [x] pnpm upstream <url> --branch <branch> accepts a branch parameter
- [x] Branch is validated against the remote repo before storing
- [x] meta.json stores branch preference in UpstreamMeta.branch field
- [x] pnpm upstream <url> --branch <branch> can be re-run to change branch
- [x] Omitting --branch continues to use repo default branch
- [x] Error handling for invalid/non-existent branches with clear messages
- [x] All code passes pnpm lint
- [x] All code passes pnpm typecheck
notes:
## Implementation Summary

### CLI Changes (src/upstream/cli.ts)
- **Branch flag parsing**: Lines 32-33 parse `--branch` (-b) flag from command arguments
- **Command definition**: Lines 90-94 define branch argument with type 'string' and alias 'b'
- **Handler**: Line 47 passes branch parameter to upstreamAdd
- **Citty integration**: Lines 103-104 pass branch to command runner

### Service Layer (src/upstream/services/git.ts)
- **InvalidBranch error**: Lines 16-20 define error class for branch validation failures
- **validateBranchExists**: Lines 74-92 validate branch exists in remote repo using `git listRemote --heads`
  - Checks for `refs/heads/{branch}` in remote references
  - Returns InvalidBranch error if not found
- **checkoutBranch**: Lines 93-143 handles branch checkout with shallow clone support
  - Discovers remote names and uses 'origin' if available
  - Uses explicit refspec for shallow clone compatibility: `+refs/heads/{branch}:refs/remotes/{remote}/{branch}`
  - Auto-unshallows if needed to access full branch history
  - Creates and checks out branch from remote tracking branch
- **addSubmodule**: Lines 24-72 adds submodule with optional branch
  - Uses shallow clone (`--depth 1`) for performance
  - Configures branch in .gitmodules via `git config -f .gitmodules`
- **setSubmoduleBranch**: Lines 144-160 updates branch configuration in .gitmodules

### Core Orchestrator (src/upstream/upstream.ts)
- **Interface updates**: Line 102 adds `branch?: string` to UpstreamAddInput
- **Schema update**: Line 127 adds optional branch field to MetaJson schema
- **setupSubmodule**: Lines 204-238
  - Validates branch before cloning (lines 214-217) - fail fast strategy
  - Clones submodule with optional branch (line 229)
  - Checks out branch for both new and existing upstreams (lines 232-236) - idempotent
- **Branch handling**: Line 283 conditionally includes branch in meta.json only when specified (using spread operator)
- **Flow**: Lines 320 and 325 thread branch through to setupSubmodule and updateMetaFile

### Key Design Decisions
1. **Fail-fast validation**: Branch validated against remote before cloning (line 216)
2. **Idempotent checkout**: Branch is checked out after both initial clone and subsequent runs (lines 232-236)
3. **Shallow clone compatible**: Uses explicit refspec and unshallow fallback for shallow clones
4. **Optional field**: Branch omitted from meta.json when not specified (line 283)
5. **Re-runnable**: Command can be re-run with different branch to switch branches

### Test Coverage (29 tests passing ✓)
- URL parsing: multiple GitHub URL formats
- Key derivation: owner/repo name resolution
- E2E add upstream: discovery and hashing
- Idempotent re-run: metadata updates
- URL normalization: consistent formatting
- Nested skill discovery: hierarchical paths
- Conflict detection: same key, different URL
- **Branch storage**: branch field in meta.json when specified
- **Branch validation**: failure handling with graceful errors
- **Branch checkout**: post-clone checkout operations
- **Idempotent re-run with branch**: actively checks out new branch on re-run
- Real git operations: end-to-end integration tests

### Quality Assurance
- ✓ All 29 tests passing
- ✓ `pnpm lint` passes
- ✓ `pnpm typecheck` passes
- ✓ Type-safe branch handling throughout

### Dependencies & Blockers
- Ready for: skills-gtp.3 (clone-skills interactive selector)
- No outstanding blockers

title: Upstream Add Command
description:
## Current State

The CLI for managing upstreams doesn't exist. Users cannot add external GitHub repos as submodules or discover available skills.

## Desired State

Users can run `pnpm upstream <url>` to add a new external GitHub repo as a git submodule, discover all SKILL.md files within it, calculate content hashes, and record the available skills in meta.json.

## User Stories

1. As a developer, I want to add a new external Upstream via pnpm upstream <github-url>, so that I can discover and select Skills from that repository
3. As a developer, I want the pnpm upstream command to discover all SKILL.md files in the Upstream, so that I can see what's available
23. As a developer, I want pnpm upstream to work with git submodules, so that Upstream code stays isolated and versioned

title: CLI: Fresh implementation of skill management commands
description:
## Current State

The CLI scripts for managing Skills (Upstreams, syncing, cleanup) are broken due to an incomplete Effect library migration. The codebase needs a fresh, clean implementation with clear behavioral contracts.

Currently, the system tracks:
- **Upstreams**: External GitHub repos (git submodules) that may contribute Skills
- **Authored Skills**: Skills owned by this repo, organized by domain
- **Sync**: Idempotent pulling and copying of Upstream-sourced Skills
- **the skill store**: Configuration store with Upstream declarations, branch tracking, and selected skill inventory

## Desired State

A clean, Effect-based CLI with seven commands that implement clear behavioral contracts:
- Users can add and manage Upstreams (declare external GitHub repos)
- Users can select which Skills to clone from each Upstream
- Users can create their own Authored Skills and have them discovered by agents
- Sync operation is robust, informative, and deterministic
- Maintenance commands (check, cleanup) are clear and predictable

All commands are invoked via pnpm scripts and fail gracefully with clear error reporting.

## User Stories

1. As a developer, I want to add a new external Upstream via `pnpm upstream <github-url>`, so that I can discover and select Skills from that repository

2. As a developer, I want to optionally specify a branch when adding an Upstream via `pnpm upstream <url> --branch <branch>`, so that I can track non-default branches

3. As a developer, I want the `pnpm upstream` command to discover all SKILL.md files in the Upstream, so that I can see what's available

4. As a developer, I want to run `pnpm clone-skills <upstream>` to get an interactive selector for which Skills to sync, so that I can choose what I need

5. As a developer, I want `pnpm clone-skills` to be re-runnable, allowing me to add or remove Skills from my selection, so that my choices can change over time

6. As a developer, I want to run `pnpm sync` to pull all Upstream branches to their correct state, discover latest Skills, and copy selected Skills to skills/, so that my local collection stays fresh

7. As a developer, I want `pnpm sync` to continue processing even if one Upstream fails, so that a single broken upstream doesn't block my workflow

8. As a developer, I want `pnpm sync` to support a --dry-run flag, so that I can see what would happen without actually modifying anything

9. As a developer, I want `pnpm sync` output to show a concise summary (per-Upstream, final totals), so that I understand what happened without overwhelming detail

10. As a developer, I want to run `pnpm check` to see how many commits behind each Upstream is, so that I know when to sync

11. As a developer, I want to run `pnpm cleanup` to automatically remove synced Skills that are no longer in the skill store, so that my skills/ directory stays clean

12. As a developer, I want to create a new Authored Skill via `pnpm new-skill <name> --domain <domain>`, so that I can organize and author my own Skills

13. As a developer, I want `pnpm new-skill` to scaffold SKILL.md and the skill store, so that I have a starting point for a new Skill

14. As a developer, I want `pnpm new-skill` to prompt me for a new name if the Skill already exists, so that I don't accidentally overwrite my work

15. As a developer, I want to run `pnpm build` to generate the claude plugin manifest from authored/ directory, so that agents can discover my Authored Skills

16. As a developer, I want the manifest to be regenerated any time I run `pnpm new-skill`, so that it stays in sync without manual steps

17. As a developer, I want the manifest to list all Authored Skills with their paths, so that external tools can discover them

18. As a developer, I want errors in any command to be clear and actionable, so that I can fix problems quickly

19. As a developer, I want the system to warn me if a Skill I selected is no longer in an Upstream, but continue processing, so that a missing Skill doesn't halt my sync

20. As a developer, I want the system to remove missing selected Skills from the skill store and delete them from skills/ during sync, so that cleanup is automatic

21. As a developer, I want each Upstream's branch to be respected during sync (checkout the declared branch), so that I can track multiple release versions

22. As a developer, I want shallow clones for all Upstreams, so that disk usage stays minimal

23. As a developer, I want `pnpm upstream` to work with git submodules, so that Upstream code stays isolated and versioned

24. As a developer, I want to manually edit the skill store to configure Upstreams, so that I have full control if needed

25. As a developer, I want the skill store structure to track: url, branch (optional), selected skills mapping, and available skills inventory with content hashes, so that all metadata is in one place

26. As a developer, I want synced Skills to be strictly read-only copies in skills/, so that I'm forced to fork Skills to authored/ if I want to modify them

27. As a developer, I want Authored Skills to live under authored/<domain>/ and be isolated from sync operations, so that my work is never overwritten

28. As a developer, I want the system to handle URL normalization (github.com/org/repo, github.com/org/repo.git, https://..., git@...), so that I don't need to worry about URL format

29. As a developer, I want cli-prompts (selectors, confirmations) to be readable and intuitive, so that interactive commands are pleasant to use

30. As a developer, I want each command to be independently testable with clear input/output contracts, so that the system is maintainable

## Out of Scope

- Editing Skills directly from the CLI (skill content management is done by hand or via external editors)
- Merging conflicting changes in Authored Skills across branches
- Publishing or contributing Skills back to Upstreams
- Real-time watching or file-system auto-sync
- Version pinning or dependency resolution between Skills
- Interactive git workflows (rebasing, cherry-picking, etc.)

## Further Notes

The implementation should live under src/ as a fresh start, replacing the current scripts/. Each command should have a well-defined orchestrator that composes lower-level effects. The skill store format is the contract between CLI commands and must remain stable across implementations.

title: Provide agent guidance for breaking epics into independent features
description: Create to-features skill to decompose epics into independent, vertically-sliced features (time-boxed). Each feature delivers complete end-to-end functionality scoped to what it addresses.

notes:
## Implementation Complete

### Artifact Created

authored/engineering/to-features/SKILL.md

### Design Finalized

**Terminology**: Feature (not sprint) — time-unboxed, independent, vertically-sliced unit of work.

**Philosophy**: Epic is decomposed into features (scoped-down versions of epic). Feature MAY deliver a deep module, MUST deliver vertical slice (complete end-to-end, independently testable).

**Principles** (borrowed from to-prd):
- Durability over precision
- Behavioral not procedural

**Process**: 5 steps (Gather context → Explore codebase → Draft vertical slices → Quiz user → Publish)

**Quiz enforces**: Complete vertical slice, granularity, dependencies, end-to-end testability

**Template**: Current State → Desired State → User Stories → Out of Scope → Further Notes (all scoped to feature)

**Key Rules**: 
- Features inherit relevant subset of epic's acceptance criteria
- Features inherit relevant subset of epic's user stories
- Vertical slice rules explicit (no horizontal slices)

### Workflow Status

Completed: to-prd (lock behavior) → to-features (decompose into features)

Next: skills-yng.3 (sprint planning guidance — lock implementation details)

title: Provide agent guidance for writing behavior-focused epics
description:

2. As an agent, I want examples of good vs bad epics so that I understand what to avoid.

3. As an agent, I want acceptance criteria templates so that I write verifiable behavior contracts.

title: Agent-handoff workflow breaks on implementation details
description:
## Current State

When I hand off complex migrations to sequential agents (plan → split into tasks → implement):
- Early planning locks down implementation details (module boundaries, data shapes, architecture)
- Agent A implements and discovers a better approach, deviates from the plan
- Agent B gets code that doesn't match the documented design
- B assumes the plan is stale, improvises independently
- Result: duplicate code, contradictory architecture, assumptions that never happened, hidden coupling

Concrete example: Effect migration planned as (primitives → domain ops → orchestrators). Once primitives shipped with different structure than planned, domain ops discovered it needed features the primitives plan said to defer. Orchestrators assumed domain ops solved X, but that X was never addressed. All tests passed. No one knew until much later.

## Desired State

I hand off work to agents sequentially and the code composes correctly, even when each agent made different implementation choices than I initially sketched. Each agent can verify their code works independently before passing it on.

When the end system behaves as I described at the start, I know I'm done.

## User Stories

1. As a project lead, I want to describe what the final system should do so that agents know what behavior to preserve, even if they implement it differently than I planned.

2. As an agent inheriting a sprint, I want to see what the previous agent built so that I can build on top of it instead of duplicating or conflicting.

3. As an agent, I want to know if I can modify previous work or only extend it, so that I don't waste time on rewrites that shouldn't happen.

4. As an agent, I want to add a function to a previous agent's module if I discover it genuinely belongs there, so that I don't duplicate it in my own code.

5. As an agent, I want to verify my code works independently (without full integration) so that I can catch problems before handing off.

6. As an agent mid-implementation, I want to realize my sprint covers two separate concerns so that I can break it apart before I'm locked into one approach.

7. As a downstream agent, I want to see what was tried before and why, so that I make better choices instead of reinventing failed approaches.

8. As a project lead, I want to know the project is done when the final system behaves as I originally described.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Sequential agents on complex migrations produce code that works together.
- [ ] Agents can verify code works without full integration.
- [ ] An agent can add to previous work without blocking handoff.
- [ ] Final system's behavior matches desired behavior described at project start.

title: Refactor scripts/ to use Effect library for superior error handling and architecture
description:
## Problem Statement

The scripts/ directory handles critical operations for the skills aggregator (sync, upstream management, cleanup, status checking) using a manual Result<T> discriminated union for error handling. While this pattern is sound, it requires:

- Manual error collection and threading through orchestration phases
- Explicit dependency passing through function signatures
- Hand-rolled side-effect management (filesystem, git, logging)
- Limited testability without global mocking
- No built-in retry, recovery, or structured observability strategies

As the codebase grows with new workflows and team contributions, this manual approach increases cognitive load and reduces code sharing.

## Solution

Migrate scripts/ to use the Effect library, introducing:

1. **Dependency Injection Layer** — Filesystem, git, logging, and process operations as composable services
2. **4-Layer Architecture** — Clear separation: CLI → Orchestrators → Domain Operations → Core Primitives
3. **Automatic Error Propagation** — Effect's error channel replaces manual threading
4. **Structured Logging** — Observability built into the service graph
5. **Testability by Design** — Every module testable via layer mocking; no global spies

This enables:
- Easier to test (inject mock implementations)
- Easier to extend (compose existing layers for new workflows)
- Easier to understand (explicit data flow, no manual threading)
- Easier to maintain (recover from errors, retry with backoff, log structured data)

## User Stories

1. As a developer, I want to test orchestrators without touching the filesystem, so that my test suite runs fast and remains deterministic

2. As a developer, I want to add a new workflow (e.g., batch rename skills), so that I can reuse existing domain operations without duplicating logic

3. As a maintainer, I want to see structured logs of what happened during sync, so that I can diagnose failures in CI/CD without re-running manually

4. As a developer, I want to understand the data flow through sync phases, so that I can confidently refactor orchestrators without introducing bugs

5. As a CI/CD operator, I want the sync command to retry git fetch on transient network failures, so that temporary outages don't block deployments

6. As a developer working offline, I want a way to mock git operations, so that I can develop sync logic on a plane

7. As a contributor adding a new Upstream integration, I want to reuse submodule-mgmt and skill-copying services, so that my code is small and focused

8. As a developer, I want custom error types for each domain (GitError, IOError, ValidationError), so that I can handle errors appropriately at each layer

9. As a maintainer, I want to swap logging backends (CLI output ↔ JSON ↔ structured telemetry), so that I can adapt to team tooling without refactoring logic

10. As a developer, I want the orchestrator to collect partial failures (some upstreams sync, others fail), so that transient upstream issues don't block the entire sync

11. As an operator, I want to measure how long each orchestrator phase takes, so that I can identify bottlenecks

12. As a developer, I want to easily write tests for skill discovery against mock directory trees, so that edge cases (nested SKILL.md, symlinks, hidden dirs) are covered

13. As a developer, I want dependency injection to be transparent in function signatures, so that I don't pass 5+ parameters through call chains

14. As a maintainer, I want the code to fail fast if core Layer 1 services are misconfigured, so that runtime errors are caught early (before orchestrators run)

15. As a developer, I want to write new domain operations (e.g., skill validation, metadata enrichment) without thinking about error types, so that Effect handles the error channel naturally

16. As a contributor, I want example code showing how to test a new orchestrator, so that I follow the pattern

17. As an operator running scripts in Docker, I want to inject a logger that writes to stdout, so that container logs are captured

18. As a developer, I want to refactor incrementally (migrate one orchestrator at a time), so that old and new code can coexist during the transition

19. As a code reviewer, I want explicit boundaries between layers, so that I can spot logic leaks (domain ops depending on CLI, for example)

20. As a developer, I want a centralized error handling strategy, so that I don't need to decide on error handling per function

## Implementation Decisions

### Module Architecture (4 Layers)

**Layer 1: Core Primitives** — Effect wrappers around Node.js APIs
-  — readFile, writeFile, mkdir, copy, symlink, remove, exists, readDir
-  — exec, getHeadSha, addSubmodule, initSubmodule, fetchSubmodule, setSubmoduleBranch, checkoutBranch, deinitSubmodule, removeSubmoduleFromGitmodules, revListCount
-  — log(level, message), indent(level)
-  — now(), iso8601()
-  — exit(code), cwd(), env(key)

**Layer 2: Domain Operations** — Business logic on top of Layer 1
-  — loadMeta, saveMeta (with validation)
-  — discoverSkills (recursive SKILL.md scanning)
-  — hashSkillDir (deterministic content hashing)
-  — copySingleSkill, writeSkillMeta
-  — collectAuthoredSkills, linkSkillToAuthoredDir, unlinkSkillFromAuthoredDir, pruneStaleLinks
-  — ensureSubmodule, updateSubmoduleBranch, removeSubmodule
-  — parseGitHubUrl, deriveUpstreamKey
-  — validateSkillName, validateDomainName, validateBranchName

**Layer 3: Orchestrators** — Workflow coordination
-  — Loop over upstreams, aggregate results, commit if changes
-  — 4-phase choreography: ensure submodule → discover → hash → copy
-  — Parse URL → derive key → check collision → ensure submodule → discover → prompt selection → sync
-  — Fetch all, count commits behind per submodule
-  — Detect orphaned skills and submodules, remove with confirmation
-  — Validate name → determine domain → create folder → write meta → symlink

**Layer 4: CLI Entry Points** — Thin presentation
-  → calls orchestrators/sync-all-upstreams.ts
-  → calls orchestrators/upstream-add.ts
-  → calls orchestrators/check.ts
-  → calls orchestrators/cleanup.ts
-  → calls orchestrators/write-skill.ts

### Error Types

-  — GitError, IOError, ProcessError, ValidationError
-  — MetaError, DiscoveryError, CopyError, SubmoduleError
-  — OrchestratorError (composes Layer 2 errors)

Errors propagate via Effect's error channel; short-circuit on fatal errors or collect partial failures (per orchestrator design).

### Dependency Injection Strategy

- All Layer 1 services provided via 
- Each orchestrator receives its input and implicitly accesses services via context
- CLI layer provides real implementations at runtime; tests provide mocks

### Orchestration Patterns

-  — Loops upstreams; uses  with error recovery (some fail, others continue)
-  — Sequential phases via  with short-circuit on failure
-  — Interactive workflow combining validation, discovery, user prompts, then sync
-  — Detects extras, prompts for confirmation, removes in bulk

### Testing Layer

- Provide mock implementations of , ,  services
- Orchestrators and domain ops test by composing real + mock layers
- CLI tests use real fixtures or E2E test suite

### Backward Compatibility

- Existing  type remains for external APIs
- Internal migration is gradual; old and new code coexist during transition
- User-facing CLI behavior unchanged (same commands, same output format)

## Testing Decisions

### What Makes a Good Test

- **Tests external behavior**, not implementation details (e.g., test that sync copies a skill, not that it calls readFile three times)
- **Fast & deterministic** — Uses mock Layer 1 services, no real I/O
- **Isolated** — Tests one orchestrator or domain op at a time, mocking its dependencies
- **Readable** — Setup → action → assertion follows a clear narrative

### Modules to Test

**Layer 1 Primitives** (tested with real Node.js APIs or mocked OS)
-  — Edge cases (missing files, permissions, deep nesting)
-  — Command failures, invalid branches, parsing edge cases
-  — All valid/invalid inputs (kebab-case, domain names, branch names)

**Layer 2 Domain Ops** (tested with mock Layer 1)
-  — Load/save malformed JSON, missing fields
-  — Nested SKILL.md, hidden dirs, symlinks, empty directories
-  — Identical directories hash identically, changed files produce different hash
-  — Copy succeeds/fails per skill, new/update/skip logic
-  — Collect authored skills with/without domains, link/unlink, prune stale
-  — All 3 states (not registered, missing dir, exists), branch updates
-  — Valid/invalid GitHub URLs, shorthand normalization

**Layer 3 Orchestrators** (tested with mock Layer 1 + Layer 2)
-  — All 4 phases succeed/fail, partial failures collected
-  — URL validation, key derivation, skill selection logic
-  — Orphan detection, confirmation flow, bulk removal
-  — Skill creation, domain handling, symlink creation

**Layer 4 CLI** (E2E tests or real fixtures)
-  — CLI parsing, output formatting, exit codes

### Testing Infrastructure

- Test fixtures: Mock Layer 1 service implementations
- Test utilities: Helpers to build test Effects, capture logs, inspect results
- Prior art: Existing  files in lib/ show pattern (unit tests + integration tests)

## Out of Scope

- Rewriting the entire test suite at once — migrate incrementally
- Changing user-facing CLI commands or output format — behavior stays the same
- Refactoring , , or ADR documentation — Effect architecture is internal
- Changes to  schema or skills directory structure
- Supporting Node.js versions < 18 (Effect has baseline requirements)
- Non-blocking: Refactoring authored skills organizational model (domain concept stays as-is)

## Further Notes

### Migration Strategy

1. **Phase 1** — Set up Effect modules + services in parallel
   - Create Layer 1 primitives (fs, git, logger, clock, process)
   - Keep existing lib/ functions unchanged; new functions use Effect

2. **Phase 2** — Migrate orchestrators one-by-one
   - Start with  (most complex; highest ROI)
   - Existing sync.ts calls orchestrators/sync-all-upstreams.ts instead of old sync-orchestrator.ts
   - Old code remains for reference

3. **Phase 3** — Migrate CLI entry points
   - Thin CLI layer calls new orchestrators
   - User-facing behavior unchanged

4. **Phase 4** — Cleanup + documentation
   - Remove old lib/ functions once migrated
   - Add tests for all new layers
   - Document architecture in AUTHORING.md or ADR

### ADR Implications

- Should create ADR-XXXX: Adopt Effect library for scripts/ orchestration
  - Rationale: Dependency injection, testability, structured error handling
  - Tradeoffs: Learning curve (Effect is non-trivial), adds runtime dependency
  - Decision: Migrate incrementally; reevaluate if team finds it valuable

### Performance Considerations

- Effect runtime overhead is minimal (nanoseconds per operation)
- Sync performance dominated by git I/O and file copying, not Effect overhead
- Structured logging (if enabled) adds negligible overhead; can be disabled

### Team Onboarding

- Provide example orchestrator + test suite as reference
- Document 4-layer architecture in AUTHORING.md
- Pair code review sessions for first PR using Effect patterns

title: Sync should fetch latest from all upstreams, even without skills defined
description: Currently, pnpm sync skips any upstream that has no 'skills' property in meta.json. This means typst and press upstreams are never updated. The script should fetch the latest commits from all registered upstreams regardless of whether they have skill selections, so the submodules stay current.

title: URL Validation & Normalization Pipeline
description:
## Problem Statement

Currently, URL handling in the scripts system is fragmented across two modules with no unified interface. The normalizeUrl() function in url-ops.ts and validateUpstreamUrl() in cli-validators.ts are called separately from different scripts (sync.ts, upstream.ts, write-skill.ts), leading to:

1. **Inconsistent URL processing**: URLs may be normalized differently depending on which script calls them
2. **Scattered validation logic**: Adding new URL formats (e.g., SSH URLs, short GitHub handles) requires changes across multiple files
3. **Poor error context**: Validation failures don't carry enough context about why a URL failed or what formats are acceptable
4. **No single source of truth**: Callers implement custom URL parsing logic inline rather than using a shared pipeline

This makes it difficult to extend URL handling consistently and increases the risk of bugs when URLs are processed differently in different workflows.

## Solution

Create a unified URLPipeline module that encapsulates all URL processing logic in a single, composable interface. This module becomes the single entry point for all URL processing in the scripts system. Callers no longer call normalizeUrl() and validateUpstreamUrl() separately; they ask the pipeline for a normalized, validated URL, and get back a strong type they can use without re-parsing.

## Key Modules to Build/Modify

1. **url.ts** (new module) - Public interface: parseAndNormalizeUrl(raw: string) → Result<NormalizedURL>. Encapsulates all URL parsing, validation, and normalization logic with no side effects.

2. **NormalizedURL** (new type) - A branded/nominal type representing a validated, normalized URL. Exposes properties: .raw, .normalized, .format, .owner, .repo

3. **url-ops.ts** (remove) - Delete this file; all functionality migrated to url.ts

4. **cli-validators.ts** (modify) - Remove public validateUpstreamUrl() function; replace call sites with url.parseAndNormalizeUrl()

5. **sync.ts, upstream.ts, write-skill.ts** (modify call sites) - Replace all calls to normalizeUrl() and validateUpstreamUrl() with url.parseAndNormalizeUrl()

## User Stories

1. As a script author, I want to validate and normalize a GitHub HTTPS URL in one call, so that I don't need to call separate validation and normalization functions
2. As a developer adding SSH URL support, I want to add that format in one place, so that all scripts automatically support it without changes elsewhere
3. As a user running pnpm upstream <url>, I want clear error messages explaining why my URL failed (e.g., Invalid format. Supported: https://github.com/org/repo, git@github.com:org/repo), so that I can correct it immediately
4. As a test author, I want to test URL handling independently, so that I can verify all supported formats work correctly without mocking sync or upstream workflows
5. As a sync script, I want to normalize upstream URLs before comparing them to existing config, so that duplicate Upstreams with different URL formats are detected
6. As the write-skill script, I want to normalize source URLs before storing them in skill metadata, so that URLs in metadata are always in a consistent format
7. As a developer maintaining URL rules, I want a central place to document what URL formats are supported, so that it's clear to future maintainers what changes are safe
8. As a developer handling URL mismatches, I want descriptive error messages that include the invalid URL and the reason, so that debugging is faster
9. As the upstream handler, I want to detect when a URL collides with an existing Upstream (despite formatting differences), so that users are prompted to disambiguate
10. As a caller, I want to use a strong NormalizedURL type instead of raw strings, so that the type system prevents accidental misuse of un-validated URLs

## Testing Approach

- **URLPipeline** (High priority) - Core module; tests cover all supported formats, error cases, and edge cases
- **Integration tests for call sites** (Medium priority) - Verify that scripts handle URL errors gracefully and display them correctly
- Prior art: cli-validators.ts validators return Result<T>; SkillMetaStore follows factory pattern with validation

## Technical Decisions

- **Canonical form**: All HTTPS URLs normalized to https://github.com/owner/repo (lowercase, no .git suffix, no trailing slash)
- **SSH support**: Accept git@github.com:owner/repo and normalize to canonical HTTPS form
- **No side effects**: Pure validation + transformation; never touches file system or git

## Out of Scope

- Webhook integration or URL resolution/connectivity testing
- URL rewriting rules for GitHub Enterprise or GitLab
- Git operations on URLs

## Further Notes

This deepening transforms shallow modules (validation + normalization in separate places) into a deep module (one place, one interface, owned responsibility). Callers go from needing to know both validateUpstreamUrl() and normalizeUrl() to just asking for one thing: 'give me a valid URL' and get back a strong type.

**Module Created:**
- scripts/lib/url.ts: Single unified entry point with parseAndNormalizeUrl()
- NormalizedURL branded type prevents misuse of unvalidated URLs

**Features Delivered:**
- HTTPS: https://github.com/owner/repo (canonical form)
- SSH: git@github.com:owner/repo (normalized to HTTPS)

- GitHub-only enforcement
- Clear, contextual error messages

**Tests:** 15 new tests covering all formats & error cases (129/129 passing)

**Call Sites Updated:**
- sync.ts: Normalize upstream URLs, eliminated redundant parsing
- upstream.ts: Use URLInfo directly (owner/repo extracted)
